column1: Stand up polisim-eda repo
column27:
Create the {{polisim-eda}} repo for exploratory data analysis on PoliSim's MVD source set (54 sources across population / electoral / opinion / context / behavioral layers — see attached MVD coverage map). EDA work itself happens in follow-up tickets; this ticket scaffolds the repo and lands the Claude Code configuration. Notebooks will be authored in VS Code.

h2. Scope

* New repo {{polisim-eda}} under the org. Python, {{uv}}-managed.
* {{src/polisim_eda/}} as an installed package ({{pip install -e .}}) — empty stubs for {{loaders/}}, {{geo/}}, {{validation/}}, {{viz/}}. Notebooks import from this package; no copy-pasted glue between notebooks.
* {{data/}} directory gitignored ({{raw/}}, {{interim/}}, {{processed/}}). {{data/README.md}} committed as the manifest.
* {{notebooks/}} directory created, empty.
* {{docs/source_lineage.md}} scaffolded — single source of truth for source provenance.
* {{docs/decisions/}} directory for ADRs.
* {{tests/}} directory with one passing smoke test.
* {{pyproject.toml}}, {{.gitignore}}, {{README.md}}, basic CI (ruff + pytest).

h2. Claude Code configuration

* {{CLAUDE.md}} at repo root encoding:
** Project framing (this is the EDA repo, feeds the composition engine, pre-MVP).
** Domain vocabulary: segment / composed stack / persona / persona agent / SimulationRun.
** Hard rules: never commit data files; every loader requires a {{docs/source_lineage.md}} entry; no Tier C ingestion without contract reference; no person-level outputs in notebooks; geography spine for all joins.
** Tooling commands: {{uv run pytest}}, {{uv run ruff check}}.
* {{.claude/settings.json}} allowlist: {{Bash(uv run *)}}, {{Bash(pytest *)}}, {{Bash(ruff *)}}, {{Read}}, {{Edit}}, {{Write}} (in-repo only). Deny writes outside repo.

h2. Acceptance criteria

* Repo created, cloneable.
* {{uv sync}} and {{pip install -e .}} work in a clean env.
* {{CLAUDE.md}} and {{.claude/settings.json}} committed.
* {{data/README.md}} and {{docs/source_lineage.md}} scaffolded (empty entries OK).
* CI green (ruff + pytest, smoke test only).

h2. Out of scope

* Any actual EDA. Source loaders, notebooks, validation logic — all follow-up tickets.
* Source ingestion or fetching.

column1: Dataset acquisition research: MVP minimum source set + ingestion priority
column27:
h2. Goal

Produce an ingestion priority ranking for the *defensible MVP minimum* dataset — the smallest source set that lets the composition → persona → persona agent → evaluator loop run end-to-end with real lineage. Broader Tier A/B/C surveys are explicitly deferred.

h2. MVP minimum source set

The scope of this ticket is locked to these sources:

* *ACS / PUMS* — population base, household constraints, demographic marginals
* *Census TIGER/Line* — geography spine (state, county, tract, block group, precinct joins downstream)
* *MIT Election Lab + VEST / Redistricting Data Hub* — electoral baseline (precinct returns, boundaries, vintage handling)
* *CES (Cooperative Election Study)* — single opinion source; strongest for subgroup/MRP-style estimates at v1
* *BLS* — economic context (LAUS at minimum; QCEW/OEWS optional if cheap to add)

That's the floor. Everything else from the legacy Tier A list (FEC, OpenSecrets, BEA, LEHD, OpenStates, CDC PLACES, NOAA, FEMA, GDELT, etc.) is *out of scope for this ticket* and lands in follow-up sprints once the loop is working.

Tier B and Tier C are out of scope entirely.

h2. Deliverable

Ranked ingestion plan for the 5 source families above, with per-source:

* Access mechanism (API, bulk download)
* Refresh cadence and vintage handling
* Effort estimate (S/M/L) for ingestion + normalization
* Geography/entity keys produced
* Validation use case (which realism check it feeds: population / electoral / opinion / context)
* Dependencies (geography spine first; everything else joins to it)

h2. Acceptance criteria

* Ingestion order defined, with TIGER geography spine sequenced first
* Per-source effort estimates (S/M/L)
* Per-source vintage/versioning approach (especially TIGER and precinct boundaries — redistricting-aware)
* Validation coverage stated: which realism tier each source unlocks
* Explicit list of deferred sources (the rest of Tier A) with one-line rationale for why each is _not_ MVP-minimum
* Top 2 sources flagged for sprint 1 (expected: TIGER + ACS/PUMS)

h2. Out of scope

* All other Tier A sources (deferred, listed but not planned)
* Tier B (state/local GIS, school board, county records)
* Tier C (licensed enrichment — separate review-gated workstream)
* Tier D (qualitative truthing)
* Schema design (separate ticket once ranking is approved)

column1: Dataset acquisition research: ingestion priority ranking across Tier A/B/C
column27:
h2. Goal

Produce an ingestion priority ranking for PoliSim datasets across Tier A (public baseline), Tier B (state/local), and Tier C (licensed enrichment), with effort estimates and access notes. This is the front-door artifact for sequencing the data backbone build.

h2. Scope

*Tier A — Public baseline (primary focus, full acquisition path):*

* ACS/PUMS, Census/TIGER
* MIT Election Lab, VEST/Redistricting Data Hub
* State and county election results
* FEC/OpenSecrets, BLS/BEA/LEHD
* OpenStates/LegiScan/NCSL/Ballotpedia
* CDC PLACES, NOAA/FEMA, GDELT/Media Cloud

*Tier B — State and local (secondary, acquisition path for pilot jurisdiction only):*

* GIS layers, state economic releases
* School board materials, county/municipal records

*Tier C — Licensed enrichment (evaluation only, NO acquisition path in this ticket):*

* Voter file enhancements (L2, Catalist, TargetSmart, etc.)
* Consumer/lifestyle panels
* Ad intelligence (AdImpact, Vivvix/CMAG)
* Audience data (Nielsen, Comscore)

Tier C entries are scoped to vendor identification, rough licensing cost bands, and contract/privacy review gates required _before_ any acquisition design begins. No procurement decisions.

h2. Deliverable

Ranked ingestion priority list with, per source:

* Tier classification
* Access mechanism (API, bulk download, scrape, license, partnership)
* Refresh cadence and vintage handling
* Effort estimate (S/M/L) for ingestion + normalization
* Geography/entity keys produced
* Validation use case (which realism check it feeds: population, electoral, opinion, salience, action)
* Privacy/legal flags (Tier C: contract + review gate notes only)
* Dependencies (e.g., geography spine before precinct returns)

h2. Acceptance criteria

* All Tier A sources ranked with effort estimates and access notes
* Tier B sources scoped to first pilot jurisdiction
* Tier C sources listed with vendors, cost bands, and explicit pre-acquisition review gates — no ingestion design
* Ranking justified by validation coverage (which sources unlock which realism tier)
* Top 5 sources flagged for sprint 1 ingestion
* Dependency graph between sources (geography spine first, then features that join to it)

h2. Out of scope

* Schema design (separate ticket once ranking is approved)
* Tier C contract negotiation or vendor selection
* Tier D qualitative truthing sources

column1: Research: Strands as agent framework, AWS Bedrock as LLM provider
column27:
Two coupled platform decisions that haven't been investigated yet:

# *Strands* as the agent-framework abstraction layer (vs. building the LLM provider interface, orchestration, and caching primitives ourselves around the existing AI-59 stub).
# *AWS Bedrock* as the LLM provider backend (vs. Anthropic-direct, vs. OpenAI, vs. self-hosted, etc.).

These are coupled because Strands has first-class Bedrock support and the framework choice partly determines what providers are cheap to integrate. They're also coupled to the result-caching question — Strands has its own opinions about caching, retries, tool-use orchestration, and request lineage; if we adopt it, we inherit those, and our app-level caching design changes accordingly.

*Why this matters now.* AI-59 is "LLMProvider interface and stub." Whatever lands there is the load-bearing abstraction that AI-63 (first real backend) and every later provider sit behind. If Strands is the right answer, AI-59's interface should be Strands-shaped from day one. If it's not, that's also fine — but we should know before we commit.

*Defense-company posture matters here.* Bedrock is plausibly the right answer specifically _because_ of this — AWS GovCloud, BAA/data-handling story, audit trail integration with CloudTrail, customer-acceptable-by-default for defense end use. Anthropic-direct is fine for development but raises questions for production deployments to defense customers. This investigation should make that posture explicit, not implicit.

h2. What to investigate

*Strands side:*

* What Strands actually is and what it abstracts (provider routing, agent orchestration, tool-use, structured outputs, caching, retries, observability).
* How well its abstractions map to PoliSim's persona-simulation pattern. Strands is built around agent-as-tool-user; PoliSim's persona agents are stance-generators, not tool-callers (today). Force the question: is Strands a good fit, or are we forcing a square peg?
* What it costs us if we adopt: lock-in, dependency surface, opinionated patterns we'd otherwise design ourselves.
* What it gives us: caching, retries, provider abstraction, possibly reduced code surface in {{polisim/llm/}}.
* Lineage and auditability story: does Strands' internal request/response tracking integrate cleanly with {{SimulationRun}} lineage, or does it create a parallel audit trail we'd have to reconcile?

*Bedrock side:*

* Available models on Bedrock (Anthropic Claude family, plus others) and how that maps to what we'd want.
* Latency, cost, throughput characteristics vs. Anthropic-direct.
* Data-handling: zero data retention options, region availability, GovCloud posture, BAA terms.
* Auth / IAM model and how it integrates with whatever environment-management posture we land on (per AI-2 / AI-28).
* Anthropic-on-Bedrock parity: do features we care about (prompt caching, tool use, vision) land on Bedrock at the same time as Anthropic-direct, or on a delay?

*Caching, specifically:*

* App-level result caching keyed on {{(persona_id, persona_version, scenario_id, scenario_version, prompt_template_id, prompt_version, model_id, model_settings_hash)}} — does Strands give us this for free, partially, or not at all?
* Bedrock-side prompt caching availability and how it compares to Anthropic-direct's prompt caching.
* Lineage implications: cache hits must show up in {{SimulationRun}} lineage, not silently substitute. How does each option (Strands, app-level, Bedrock-native) handle this?

h2. Deliverables

* {{docs/research/strands-bedrock-evaluation.md}} covering:
** Strands evaluation: what it does, where it fits PoliSim, where it doesn't, lock-in cost.
** Bedrock evaluation: provider mechanics, defense-posture story, parity with Anthropic-direct, pricing/latency notes.
** Caching analysis: which layer should own result caching given the framework + provider choices, and what lineage looks like in each scenario.
** Recommendation: adopt Strands / don't, use Bedrock / don't, with reasoning.
* *If recommendation is "adopt":* ADR draft ({{docs/adr/00NN-llm-provider-strands-bedrock.md}}) locking the choice, plus follow-up tickets to refactor AI-59's interface accordingly.
* *If recommendation is "don't adopt" or "defer":* the research note alone is the deliverable. No ADR. Capture the reasoning for revisiting later.

h2. Done when

* Research note merged to {{dev}}.
* Recommendation is concrete (adopt/defer/reject for each of Strands and Bedrock independently — they can be answered separately).
* ADR + follow-up tickets filed if recommendation is adopt.

h2. Dependencies

* Useful but not blocking: AI-59 (LLMProvider interface stub) merged. Investigation can run on the existing stub design.
* Useful but not blocking: AI-63 (first real backend) — the investigation may _be_ the input that decides which backend AI-63 implements.

h2. Out of scope

* Implementing the chosen framework or provider. Follow-up tickets if adopted.
* Building any caching layer. Strictly evaluation here.
* Comparing every possible agent framework. Strands is the candidate; if the answer is "neither Strands nor home-grown is right," flag that and we open a separate broader investigation.
* Bedrock provisioning, IAM setup, GovCloud account work. Operational, follows the decision.

Priority: Medium. Backlog, but worth landing before AI-63 commits to a specific provider. Best-fit owner: Elijah (LLM domain, post-arrival 2026-05-04).
column53:
06/May/26 4:47 PM;712020:00faf8cf-dd11-46fb-98ae-3bf313916d7c;Initial framing input from a research session with Claude (2026-05-06). One informed perspective; the deliverable described in this ticket is still owed.

*Lean: adopt Strands as the runtime framework, Bedrock as the LLM provider.*

h2. Why Strands

The conversation walked through DSPy vs Strands as the primary runtime spine. First-pass conclusion was DSPy-as-library inside our {{LLMClient}}, on the strength of typed signatures and the prompt-optimization story (GEPA, MIPROv2). That conclusion didn't survive two arguments:

# *Smaller-model grounding will likely require runtime tool use.* Product trajectory points at locally-hosted open-weight models (per CDM §9.2 / handoff §9). Smaller models don't reliably produce defensible structured outputs from a single shot — they need to retrieve evidence packs mid-reasoning, not just receive them pre-loaded by the composition layer. That's the agent-loop case, not the single-shot case the canonical data model describes today. DSPy has {{ReAct}} for this but it's the less-mature corner of DSPy; Strands' agent loop is the framework's product surface.
# *Defense-posture fit with the AWS stack is not orthogonal to the framework choice.* Strands' default path is Bedrock + GovCloud + CloudTrail + IAM. DSPy is provider-agnostic via LiteLLM but pulls toward direct-API integrations that are harder to defend in a defense-customer audit conversation.

h2. Where Strands fits PoliSim's shape

* Pydantic-validated structured output covers {{AgentOutput}} schema needs.
* OTel-native observability matches the auditability requirement and integrates cleanly with whatever observability stack we land on.
* Graph primitive maps to compose → simulate → evaluate → synthesize without forcing the model-driven loop on stages that should be deterministic.
* Hooks (15+ lifecycle events) give us the seam where prompt versioning, run-lineage capture, and RLS-session-binding land, keeping {{LLMClient}} as the architectural chokepoint per {{CLAUDE.md}}.

h2. Why Bedrock

Three concrete reasons, in priority order:

# *Defense-customer audit story.* GovCloud availability, BAA terms, CloudTrail integration with IAM, region pinning. Anthropic-direct is fine for development but raises questions for production deployments to defense customers. Bedrock removes the question.
# *Model variety from one provider.* Claude family + Nova + Llama + Mistral + Cohere + Titan, all behind one IAM model and one billing relationship. The two-tier model strategy in the handoff (frontier model for synthesis, cheaper model for high-volume persona drafts) becomes a routing decision inside one provider rather than a multi-vendor integration problem.
# *Out-of-the-box inference + cost-competitive.* No standing inference infrastructure to manage, pay-per-token across all models, prompt caching available on Anthropic-on-Bedrock. Pending the comparative analysis this ticket calls for, expected to be cost-competitive with direct-API and self-hosted alternatives at our expected volume — until we're committing to fine-tuned local models, which is itself deferred.

h2. Audit-story shift this implies

Multi-step persona reasoning changes what "the prompt sent to the model" means. The replayable artifact becomes the trace: system prompt version + tool definitions version + sequence of model decisions and tool returns. CDM §5.3 / §5.4 still works but {{raw_llm_output}} should hold the full OTel trace JSON, not a single rendered prompt. Replays at temperature > 0 reconstruct _what happened_, not _reproduce the output deterministically_. Most enterprise audit requirements actually want the former, but the doc should make this explicit before code lands. Probably warrants its own ADR.

h2. Constraints if Strands is adopted (worth being explicit in the ADR)

* *In-bounds:* {{Agent}} with tools, {{Graph}} for stage gating, hooks for versioning + lineage capture.
* *Out-of-bounds:* {{Swarm}} (emergent, hard to audit), Meta-agents (agents that modify their own orchestration — structurally incompatible with versioned prompts).

Without explicit constraints in {{CLAUDE.md}} or {{polisim/agents/README.md}}, contributors will reach for swarm and meta-agent primitives "because they're there."

h2. Things to validate during this ticket's research

* *Smaller-model-grounding hypothesis.* A spike with a Mistral-class or Llama-class model under static grounding (composition layer pre-loads evidence) would tell us whether the agent loop is load-bearing or single-shot is sufficient. If single-shot works, much of the multi-agentic case weakens — though the Bedrock case stands on its own.
* *Caching layer ownership* (the under-explored question this ticket already calls out). Strands' opinions about caching/retries/lineage need to be reconciled with our app-level result-caching design before commit.
* *AI-59's* {{LLMProvider}} ABC. If Strands is adopted, AI-59 should be Strands-shaped from day one. If Strands is deferred, AI-59 stands as designed. Sequencing matters.

h2. Out of scope for this ticket (separate research tickets to follow)

* *Automated Prompt Optimization (APO)* — separate ticket. Question of what optimizer (GEPA, TextGrad, PromptWizard, manual + LLM-as-judge) to layer over Strands' system prompts once enough analyst-validated examples accumulate. Different timeline (~6 months out, depends on data accumulation). Doesn't gate this ticket.
* *Preference fine-tuning / model adaptation* (ORPO, DPO, SimPO, continued pretraining) — separate ticket. Different layer entirely (model weights, not prompts), different timeline (Phase 5+ per handoff), different hardware path (the dual Nvidia Sparks per CDM §9.2). Doesn't gate this ticket.
column54:
06/May/26 4:58 PM;712020:00faf8cf-dd11-46fb-98ae-3bf313916d7c;*Flag for the Strands evaluation: Skills-feature activation reliability is model-dependent, and the gap is empirical, not documented.*

Adding this because the ticket assumes Anthropic-on-Bedrock parity, but if non-Anthropic Bedrock models (Llama, Mistral, Nova, Titan) are in scope at any point — even for cost-tier fallback or local-dev parity with Ollama — the Skills feature has a failure mode worth knowing about.

h2. Background

Strands' {{AgentSkills}} plugin implements the AgentSkills.io spec: progressive disclosure (metadata in system prompt → SKILL.md loaded on activation → resources on demand). It offers three activation patterns:

* *File-based* — implicit, model "just knows" from system-prompt metadata
* *Tool-based* — explicit {{skill(skill_name=...)}} tool call
* *Meta-Tool* — each skill is an isolated sub-agent exposed as a tool

Activation reliability (does the model recognize when to load a skill, and follow it once loaded) is empirically model-dependent. Anthropic models handle it well; smaller / non-Anthropic models can fail to activate or fail to adhere — silently, with no observable signal in the File-based pattern.

h2. What's missing

No off-the-shelf eval covers this specifically. Adjacent benchmarks are weak proxies:

* BFCL / τ-bench / Nexus — tool-calling competence (Tool-based activation is a tool call, so this proxies)
* IFEval / FollowBench / InfoBench — instruction adherence after the skill is loaded
* RULER — long-context behavior as the metadata block grows with skill count

If we adopt Strands Skills against multi-model Bedrock, we should build a custom eval. Estimate: 1–2 days with {{promptfoo}} or Inspect AI as the harness.

* 50–200 prompts per skill (should-trigger, should-not-trigger, ambiguous, adversarial-mention)
* Log activation event, skill chosen, adherence (deterministic checks where possible; pairwise LLM-as-judge where not)
* Compare candidate model vs. Claude baseline vs. small-model floor
* Cheap pre-eval diagnostic: ~20 prompts in each of the three patterns, eyeball results — tells us whether to invest further or reconsider the abstraction

h2. Pattern recommendation if non-Anthropic models are in scope

Prefer *Tool-based* or *Meta-Tool* over File-based.

* File-based gives no observable activation event — failures are silent, can't be graded.
* Tool-based produces a gradeable activation call.
* Meta-Tool collapses skill selection into standard tool selection (BFCL-style scores apply directly), at the cost of more tokens per skill load.

For Bedrock models below ~70B parameter scale, I'd be skeptical of the Skills abstraction at all — a structured workflow with LLM nodes, or fine-tuning for specific domains, is likely more reliable than betting on instruction-following.

h2. Scope question

This is only relevant to the ticket if multi-model Bedrock is actually being considered. If the working assumption is Claude-on-Bedrock only, dismiss this. If cost-tier fallback to Nova/Llama or local-dev parity with Ollama is on the table, the eval cost should be priced into the Strands adoption decision — not discovered post-adoption.

Not blocking. Filing for visibility.

column1: Research: data sources for grounding and evaluating constituency simulations
column27:
Two open questions, deeply linked, neither of which has been seriously investigated yet:

# What real-world data sources exist that could ground PoliSim's {{JurisdictionFeature}} rows, persona composition, and scenario context?
# What evaluations can we run against those data sources — i.e., how do we know PoliSim's outputs reflect anything real?

These are the same investigation. Sources without a theory of how they validate the simulation are just inventory; evaluation ideas without sources are speculation. Both halves together are what's actually useful.

*This is research, not an ADR or implementation.* No locked decision required. The deliverable is "we understand the landscape and have a defensible point of view on what's worth pursuing." Specific decisions (which sources to ingest first, which evaluations to build) become their own tickets if and when the research surfaces enough conviction to commit.

h2. What to investigate

*Sources side:*

* Public/free authoritative data: ACS, Decennial Census, BLS, NCES, FCC, FEC, state-level (Utah AGRC, Utah DWS), county portals.
* Election returns and turnout data — granularity, latency, coverage.
* Licensed voter files (L2, TargetSmart, Catalist) — what they contain, commercial-use terms (defense-company posture is non-standard), cost.
* Qualitative truthing sources — local journalism archives, civic org reports, ethnographic studies, polling archives.
* For each: what it provides, how it's accessed, how often it updates, what it costs, what restrictions it carries.

*Evaluations side — the harder, more important half:*

* What does it mean for a PoliSim output to be "right"? A persona's predicted stance on a defense-spending scenario — what real-world signal could we compare against? Polling data, town-hall comments, social-media-derived signals, election-result inference, qualitative interviews?
* For each candidate evaluation: what data source supports it, what level of granularity is needed, what the noise floor looks like, whether ground truth even exists at the persona/segment level we operate at.
* Which evaluations are tractable in the next 6 months vs. multi-year research projects.
* Where there is _no_ ground truth available — flag this honestly. Some claims PoliSim makes are unfalsifiable with current data; that's worth knowing.
* How this connects to AI-64 (persona-reaction prompt tests and eval harness seed) — what the harness _could_ test if it had real data behind it.

h2. Deliverables

* {{docs/research/data-sources-and-evaluations.md}} — research note covering:
** Source landscape (Tier A/B/C/D framing per Engineering Handoff §11 is fine as a starting structure).
** Evaluation landscape: candidate evaluation approaches, what they'd need, what they'd prove.
** Honest assessment of where ground truth exists and where it doesn't.
** A point of view: which 2–3 sources and which 1–2 evaluation approaches are worth pursuing first, with reasoning. Not a locked decision — a recommendation.
* Open questions captured for product / leadership input (granularity, customer-vs-public-data posture, Tier C licensing, etc.).
* Follow-up tickets filed only if the research surfaces concrete next steps worth committing to. If the honest answer is "we need to think more," that's a fine ending — the doc captures that and we revisit.

h2. Done when

* Research note merged to {{dev}}.
* Open questions section is concrete enough that someone (you, CEO, PM) can act on it.
* Any follow-up tickets filed.

h2. Dependencies

None hard. AI-77 in flight is useful context (the hand-written Davis-Weber baseline is what real ingest would replace).

h2. Out of scope

* ETL pipeline implementation. Not even sketched here.
* Schema changes. If the research suggests a {{JurisdictionFeature}} slot is missing, file a separate ticket; don't change the model as part of this work.
* Customer-uploaded evidence (AI-66 handles that path).
* Building any actual evaluation. Designing the approach is the deliverable; building it is downstream.
* Locking sourcing or evaluation decisions. This is research; commitment comes later.

Priority: Medium-High. Backlog. Best-fit owner: TBD (could be Kevin pre-Elijah; eval angle is genuinely Elijah-shaped post-arrival).
column53: 07/May/26 1:17 PM;712020:00faf8cf-dd11-46fb-98ae-3bf313916d7c;Research note for Datasets is included in the polisim-eda repo.

column1: Squash `0002_enable_pgvector` into `0001` baseline; document extension provisioning as init.sql-owned
column27:
Migration {{0002_enable_pgvector}} exists in the alembic chain but does not actually create or drop the {{vector}} extension in any environment we run today. {{infra/docker/postgres/init.sql:36}} creates the extension as the bootstrap superuser before alembic ever runs; the migration's {{upgrade()}} is a documented "clean no-op" ({{CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector}} against an already-present extension), and after [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|smart-link] the {{downgrade()}} is also an explicit no-op because {{polisim_migrations}} cannot drop an extension it does not own and Postgres provides no path to transfer extension ownership.

The result is a migration revision that models an extension lifecycle it does not actually own. The architecturally honest answer is that the migration should not exist - {{vector}} is provisioned by infra, exclusively, and the alembic chain should reflect that.

This ticket squashes {{0002}} into the {{0001}} baseline so the migration chain stops carrying a vestigial revision. The behavior change is zero (both upgrade and downgrade are already no-ops post-[https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|smart-link]); this is purely a chain-shape correction.

Deliverables

* Remove {{backend/src/polisim/db/migrations/versions/0002_enable_pgvector.py}} from the migration chain.
* Update {{0001}}'s baseline to document - in a comment block at the top - that the {{vector}} extension is provisioned by {{infra/docker/postgres/init.sql}}, is owned by the bootstrap superuser, and is intentionally not part of the migration chain. Reference ADR 0005's role-separation posture and [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|smart-link]'s verification finding (no {{ALTER EXTENSION ... OWNER TO}} in Postgres; pgvector is non-trusted).
* Update the {{down_revision}} field on {{0003}} (currently {{0002}}) to point to {{0001}}.
* Plan an alembic stamp ritual for any environment that has already applied {{0002}}. For local dev, {{make reset-db}} handles it (drops the volume entirely; the new chain runs cleanly from scratch). For higher environments, document the {{alembic stamp}} sequence in {{docs/runbooks/}} so an operator can rebase a populated DB onto the squashed chain without re-running migrations.
* Sanity-check that no other migration's {{down_revision}} chain references {{0002}} directly. If any do, retarget.
* Verify the round-trip from a fresh DB: {{make reset-db}} → {{alembic upgrade head}} → {{alembic downgrade base}} → {{alembic upgrade head}} all clean, with the new chain shape.
* Sanity-check {{architecture-diagrams.md}} and any other doc that references migration {{0002}} by number; update or remove as appropriate.

Done when

* {{0002_enable_pgvector.py}} no longer exists in the repo.
* {{0001}}'s baseline carries the documented rationale for why pgvector is not in the migration chain.
* Round-trip from a fresh DB is clean against the new chain shape.
* A runbook exists at {{docs/runbooks/}} (path TBD by implementer) describing the stamp procedure for environments that have already applied {{0002}}. Local-dev path is {{make reset-db}}.
* No dangling {{down_revision = "0002..."}} references anywhere in the migration chain.
* Pre-commit clean.

Dependencies

* [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|smart-link] merged. The squash assumes the no-op {{downgrade()}} is in place; without it, the migration's behavior is non-trivially different and the squash carries more risk.
* Soft coupling to [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-28|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-28|smart-link] (hosting decision). Higher-environment stamp procedure depends on what those environments look like; if [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-28|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-28|smart-link] hasn't landed when this ticket is picked up, the runbook can document local-dev only and flag higher-environment stamping as TBD.

Out of scope

* Any change to {{infra/docker/postgres/init.sql}}'s extension-creation behavior. Init.sql is the source of truth for {{vector}} and stays so.
* Refactoring how other migrations are structured. This is a {{0002}}-specific squash justified by {{0002}}'s no-op-on-both-ends shape; other migrations remain as-is.
* Adding fresh-baseline downgrade tests to CI. [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|smart-link] named that out-of-scope; same rationale here.
* Reviewing all other migrations for similar "vestigial revision" patterns. None exist today; this is a {{vector}}-specific issue because extensions have ownership semantics that tables don't.
* Changes to ADR 0005. The role-separation posture is correct; this ticket aligns the migration chain with it rather than changing it.

Priority

Low. Architectural cleanup, not a bug fix. The bug is closed by [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-99|smart-link]; this ticket exists to remove the vestigial revision so the migration chain is honest about what it owns. Backlog candidate. Reasonable to fast-track if [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-28|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-28|smart-link] hosting work needs the higher-environment stamp procedure documented anyway, or if a fresh-baseline-downgrade CI check ever lands and starts catching the no-op as noise.

column1: README first-clone setup flow: document `make migrate` between `make dev` and `make dev-apps`
column27:
The "Run the local stack" section in root README (post-[https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-43|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-43|smart-link]) leads with {{make dev}} for db-only and {{make dev-apps}} for the full stack, but does not tell a fresh-clone reader they need to run {{make migrate}} between the two before the api will function against a real schema.

The architectural choice (migrations stay a separate explicit step rather than getting wrapped into {{make dev-apps}}) is intentional and correct: container lifecycle and schema state are different concerns, migration failures should be loud and recoverable as their own command, and the daily-driver flow is two commands ({{make dev-apps}}, work) rather than re-running {{alembic upgrade head}} on every up. The architectural choice is not in scope for this ticket. The docs gap is.

Net: a fresh-clone reader following the README today does not know {{make migrate}} exists, when to run it, or that they need to.

Deliverables

* Add a step to the "Run the local stack" section walking a fresh-clone reader through the one-time setup sequence: {{cp infra/.env.example infra/.env}}, {{make dev}}, {{make migrate}}, {{make dev-apps}}. Make it visually distinct from the daily-driver flow so a returning reader doesn't think they need to re-run all four every time.
* Mention {{make migrate}} by name and link to or briefly describe what it does (apply pending Alembic migrations against the running db). The current README references {{make migrate}} only later in the "Database and migrations" section (~L93/L102-103) as a precondition assumed already understood.
* Decide whether the daily-driver flow ({{make dev-apps}} alone, after first-time setup) gets its own subsection or stays as the assumed default with one-time setup called out as the exception. Either shape works; pick whichever reads cleaner against the existing section structure.
* Sanity-check {{infra/README.md}} for the same gap. The "Bring up" section there post-[https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-43|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-43|smart-link] reads {{make dev}} / {{make dev-apps}} and may carry the same omission.
* Sanity-check {{CONTRIBUTING.md}}'s pointer to "README §Run the local stack" - if the README section gets a meaningful restructure, the cross-reference should still land cleanly.

Done when

* A fresh-clone reader following the README's "Run the local stack" section top-to-bottom can bring up the full stack against a migrated db without needing to know {{make migrate}} exists in advance.
* The one-time-setup vs. daily-driver distinction is visually clear.
* {{infra/README.md}} and {{CONTRIBUTING.md}} cross-references remain coherent.
* Pre-commit clean.

Dependencies

* [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-43|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-43|smart-link] merged (the README sections this ticket edits were last touched there).

Out of scope

* Wrapping {{make migrate}} into {{make dev-apps}}. The architectural separation is intentional; that's a closed decision.
* Adding a new {{make first-time-setup}} or {{make bootstrap}} wrapper that runs the four-command sequence end-to-end. Possibly worth it; possibly over-engineered for a one-time flow. File as a separate ticket if it becomes friction.
* AI-20-era staleness on root README L11/L15/L85-86 ([https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-101|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-101|smart-link]'s territory).
* Restructuring or expanding the "Database and migrations" section beyond what's needed to keep its cross-references coherent with the rewritten "Run the local stack" section.
* Any code changes. Pure docs.

Priority

Low. Pure documentation gap; not blocking any sprint goal. Backlog candidate; cheap to batch with [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-101|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-101|smart-link] if both land in the same Sprint 3 slot.

* {{README.md}} lines 9-15 still say "FastAPI service (scaffolding not yet initialized)" and "Next.js + TypeScript web app (scaffolding not yet initialized)" with the line "the api and web services are defined but will not start until the backend and frontend scaffolding land (tracked as [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-15|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-15|smart-link] and [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-20|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-20|smart-link])". Both have clearly landed - {{frontend/package.json}} is a real Next 16 app, {{backend/}} has six migrations through {{0006_run_lineage}}, and {{infra/README.md}} documents bringing up db + api together. A first-time reader sees contradictory framing.
* {{docs/runbooks/local-db-reset-for-rls.md}} line 76 says {{# Expect: 0003_create_organizations}} after {{make reset-db}}. Current head is {{0006_run_lineage}} (three migrations of drift). Anyone following the runbook's verification step today will think their stack is broken.

Both are mechanical corrections - no architectural decisions, no rewrites.

Deliverables

* {{README.md}} lines 9-15: replace the "scaffolding not yet initialized" framing and the [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-15|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-15|smart-link]/ [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-20|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-20|smart-link]-pending line with current state - backend FastAPI scaffold and frontend Next.js scaffold both landed; {{db}} + {{api}} profile bring-up documented in {{infra/README.md}}.
* {{docs/runbooks/local-db-reset-for-rls.md}} line 76: update the expected migration head from {{0003_create_organizations}} to {{0006_run_lineage}} (or, better, replace the literal name with {{current head}} to avoid this drift recurring).

Done when

* Both files reflect current repo state.
* No other content changes - this is a drift-correction ticket, not a doc rewrite.

Dependencies

* None.

Out of scope

* Any other doc drift not specifically named above (a broader sweep belongs with [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-104|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-104|smart-link] when it lands).
* Any restructuring of {{README.md}} or the runbook beyond the specific lines named.

column1: Add Makefile wrapper for the apps-profile compose up
column27:
{{make dev}} brings up only the DB container. The API container requires {{cd infra && docker compose --profile apps up -d}}, which is documented in {{make help}}'s parenthetical ({{apps via --profile apps}}) but not exposed as a Makefile target.

Operator-friction: a new engineer reading {{make help}} for the first time has to parse the parenthetical, navigate to {{infra/}}, and remember the compose-profile syntax to bring the full stack up. [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-43|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-43|smart-link] added the apps profile but did not wrap it. With the team phase starting 2026-05-04 and Elijah onboarding, the missing wrapper is now a real friction surface.

Surfaced 2026-05-04 during [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-95|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-95|smart-link] manual verification: {{make dev}} brought only the DB up, {{curl localhost:8000/readyz}} returned nothing, and the path forward required reading {{make help}} and reconstructing the compose-profile invocation manually.

Deliverables

* Add {{make dev-apps}} (or the team's preferred name - match the prevailing Makefile convention; {{make help}} shows lowercase-hyphenated targets) that wraps {{cd infra && docker compose --profile apps up -d}}.
* Update {{make help}} text to surface the new target alongside {{make dev}}. Optionally tighten the existing {{make dev}} description so the relationship is clear ({{make dev}} brings up DB only; {{make dev-apps}} brings up DB + API).
* Verify the new target idempotently brings the stack to the apps-profile state from any starting state (DB-only, all-down, or already-up).
* Update {{infra/README.md}} if it currently documents the manual {{cd infra && docker compose --profile apps up -d}} invocation - the README should now point at the wrapper.

Done when

* {{make dev-apps}} brings up DB + API cleanly from a stopped state.
* {{make help}} lists the new target with a one-line description.
* {{infra/README.md}} reflects the wrapper if it documented the manual form.
* A new engineer reading {{make help}} cold can bring up the full stack without reading the parenthetical or navigating to {{infra/}}.

Dependencies

* [https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-43|https://kenaidefense.atlassian.net/browse/AI-43|smart-link] merged (apps profile exists in {{docker-compose.yml}}).

Out of scope

* Restructuring the existing {{make dev}} target's behavior. The DB-only default stays - that is the minimal-footprint mode used for migration work and integration tests.
* Adding a {{make dev-frontend}} or other-profile wrappers. Only the apps profile is in scope here; further wrappers if/when other profiles emerge.
* Refactoring the Makefile's compose-invocation pattern more broadly.

Priority

Low. DX hygiene; not blocking any sprint goal. Sprint 3 candidate.
